feedback.text: To whom it may concern, I hope this message finds you well. I would like to raise a concern regarding the increasing trend of games becoming inaccessible after the end of their service period. I believe this issue falls under misleading marketing practices by influencers and publishers, as the limited lifespan of these games is rarely, if ever, disclosed to the public at the time of sale. By failing to clearly state that a game is a "live service" with a finite duration, and instead presenting it as a permanent product, consumers are misled. This misrepresentation can have serious consequences. A simple solution would be for marketers and advertisers to clearly communicate whether a title is a game-as-a-service or a standalone product. If it is a service, the expected duration of that service should be transparently disclosed before purchase. For example, "Anthem" by BioWare (published by Electronic Arts) has already been taken offline. Many consumers, including those who purchased the game at launch six years ago, were never informed that it would be entirely inaccessible after a certain point. Though it was functionally a service, it was marketed and sold as a productone that consumers expected to own permanently. While I am not a legal expert, this kind of practice feels ethically wrong, if not outright deceptive. Currently, I find myself questioning the future of "Elite Dangerous" by Frontier Developments. What happens when the publisher no longer profits from it? Will it be shut down as well? I purchased what I believed to be a product, but I may have unknowingly paid for a temporary service. If core functionalities are removed or the game is shut down entirely, I, like many others, will be left with nothing despite having paid full price. As someone who truly values the games I play, I hope to continue enjoying them regardless of whether the publisher still sees them as profitable. Transparency in how games are marketed and sold is not just a courtesyits a responsibility to the consumer. Thank you for taking the time to consider my feedback. Sincerely, A concerned consumer and citizen.
